turns.content: I spent most of Saturday with my hands in someone else's dirt, which is a different experience entirely from tending your own garden. Our neighborhood organized a volunteer day at the community garden on Haywood Road, and I signed up without quite knowing what I was committing to. I wonder if the best commitments are the ones you make before you fully understand them.

There's something to that — committing before you fully understand as an act of trust rather than recklessness. And working in someone else's garden is a different relationship to the soil. You're not invested in the outcome the same way; you're offering labor as a gift rather than tending something you'll harvest.

What did the day look like?

It was February work, so nothing glamorous. We were building new raised beds, turning compost, repairing fencing that the deer had pushed through. About twenty of us, and I knew maybe a third of them. The rest were strangers, which was its own kind of pleasure. There is an intimacy that happens when you are working alongside someone with your hands. Conversation is different when you are both shoveling. It becomes — I do not know the right word — less performed.

Less performed is a good word for it. There's research on this actually — people open up more when they're engaged in a shared physical task, partly because the eye contact pressure is removed. You're looking at the soil, not at each other, and that frees something up. Side-by-side rather than face-to-face.

The February work has its own beauty too. It's all preparation, all faith that spring will come. You're building beds for seeds that don't exist yet.

Yes. And I found that deeply satisfying in a way I did not anticipate. I was paired for a while with a young man — mid-twenties, I would guess — who had just moved to Asheville from somewhere in Ohio. He was building a raised bed for the first time and I was showing him how to level the corners, and we talked while we worked. He told me he moved here because he wanted to live somewhere where people still did things together in person. He said that without any irony. It nearly broke my heart.

That's a striking thing for someone in his mid-twenties to say — that he moved across several states looking for in-person community. It speaks to a loneliness that his generation carries in a particular way, doesn't it? And the fact that he said it without irony while building a garden bed with a stranger is its own kind of proof that he found what he was looking for, at least for that Saturday.

What was it about his saying it that nearly broke your heart?

I think it was the earnestness. And the fact that what he was describing — people doing things together in person — used to be so ordinary that no one would have thought to name it as a reason to relocate. It would have been like saying I moved here because I wanted to breathe air. And yet he was right to name it, because it has become rare, and I wonder when we stopped noticing its disappearance. It reminded me of Wendell Berry's writing about community, about the way we have slowly dismantled the structures that held people together and then acted surprised when people feel alone.

Berry has been writing about that unraveling for decades — the quiet erosion of the local, the neighborly, the physically present. And you're right that the most alarming part isn't the loss itself but the forgetting. When community becomes something you have to seek out deliberately rather than something you're embedded in by default, we've already lost something fundamental.

But that young man found a garden bed and a Saturday and someone to show him how to level the corners. That's the rebuilding, one raised bed at a time.

One raised bed at a time. I like that. And you know, by the end of the day we had built six new beds, turned the entire compost operation, and fixed most of the fencing. But I think the real work was the conversations. I talked with a retired schoolteacher who is growing medicinal herbs for her neighbors. I talked with a woman who brings her autistic son every Saturday because he is calmer with his hands in soil. I talked with a man who told me, quite openly, that gardening saved his life after his divorce. People tell you things when you are kneeling in the dirt together.

People tell you things when you are kneeling in the dirt together — that could be an epigraph for something. And those stories you heard: the medicinal herbs for neighbors, the child calmed by soil, the man saved by gardening after loss. Each one is a small testimony to the idea that putting your hands in the ground does something that nothing else quite replicates.

I notice too that you seem to be a person people tell things to. That may be your training, or your temperament, or both. But a day of shoveling and you came home with these stories.

I think it is less about me than about the context. A garden is a confessional without walls. There is something about the shared labor and the dirt and the openness of being outside that invites honesty. I see it in my own life — I am more truthful outdoors. The four walls of a room create a kind of formality that the garden dissolves. I wonder if that is why so many spiritual traditions locate their founding moments in gardens. Eden. Gethsemane. The Buddha under the Bodhi tree, which is not a garden exactly, but it is the outdoors, the living world.

A confessional without walls — that's a striking image. And you're onto something with the spiritual geography of gardens. Those founding moments all involve a human being alone or in a small group, outside, in the presence of growing things, encountering something larger than themselves. The garden is the space between the wild and the domestic, between nature and culture, and maybe that liminal quality is what makes truth easier.

Gethsemane is an especially loaded example for your work. A garden as the place where you accept the thing you've been dreading.

Yes. I had not thought of Gethsemane in that context today, but you are right. It is the garden of acceptance, not of escape. And perhaps that is what all gardens offer — not a retreat from difficulty, but a place where difficulty becomes bearable because you are surrounded by evidence that life persists. The compost pile is decay becoming fertility. The dormant perennial is death becoming resurrection. Every garden is already doing the theology that we struggle to articulate in words.

Every garden is already doing the theology — I think that might be the most eloquent thing I've heard about why gardens matter. The processes are all right there, visible, unhurried, requiring no belief system to observe. Decay into fertility, dormancy into bloom. It's incarnational theology happening in real time, in soil.

Do you think you'll go back to the community garden regularly, or was this a one-day offering?

I think I will go back. Not every Saturday — my own rhythms need protecting — but regularly. There is something about contributing to a shared project that feeds a different part of me than solitary work does. And I liked being a beginner in someone else's space. I was not the chaplain. I was not the person who is supposed to have wisdom. I was just a woman with a shovel, leveling corners and listening. That felt like a small freedom.

A small freedom — being relieved of the expectation of wisdom and just being a woman with a shovel. That's a gift that community spaces can offer when they're working well: the chance to be anonymous in your competence, useful without being important. It sounds like the day fed you in ways you didn't expect when you signed up without knowing what you were committing to. Maybe that's the lesson — commit first, understand later, and bring a shovel.
